parameters.name: Tesla Optimus mass (company claim)
parameters.source: Tesla Optimus Gen 2 unveil, 2023-12-12 (company claim, hardware shown); carried into Gen 3/V3 statements through 2026
parameters.notes: 125 lb / 173 cm. V3 mass not separately stated as of 2026-07; range allows growth for mass-production body. Company claim, not independently weighed.

parameters.name: Tesla Optimus battery capacity (company claim)
parameters.source: Tesla AI Day, 2022-09-30 (company claim): 2.3 kWh, 52 V pack
parameters.notes: V3 pack size unstated as of 2026-07; upper range allows a larger production pack. Usable energy will be ~80-90% of nameplate.

parameters.name: Optimus runtime per charge (claim vs realistic)
parameters.source: Tesla claim ('full work day' ~8 h, light tasks, AI Day 2022 + later); independent humanoid-deployment surveys show 2.5-4 h under sustained load
parameters.notes: 8 h consistent with 2.3 kWh only at ~290 W average (light duty). Use 3 h/charge for heavy Mars work in L1/L2 models. No independent 8 h demonstration exists as of 2026-07.

parameters.name: Optimus carry payload (company claim)
parameters.source: Tesla AI Day 2022 + subsequent statements: ~20 kg (45 lb) carry; ~68 kg (150 lb) deadlift claim repeated for Gen 3
parameters.notes: Company claim; light-object handling demonstrated, 68 kg deadlift never publicly shown. Mars gravity (0.379 g) roughly doubles-to-triples effective carriable mass at same joint torque, minus dynamic stability limits.

parameters.name: Optimus walking speed (demonstrated / claimed)
parameters.source: Gen 2 video 2023-12-12 (~0.6 m/s, '30% faster'); V3 claim 5 mph = 2.2 m/s (Musk statements 2025-2026, company claim)
parameters.notes: Use 0.5-0.8 m/s for Mars planning (unstructured terrain, conservative gait); 2.2 m/s is an undemonstrated claim. Compare flight rovers: 0.01-0.05 m/s.

parameters.name: Optimus hand degrees of freedom (company claim)
parameters.source: Gen 3 hand claim: 22 DoF hand/forearm, tactile fingertips (We,Robot 2024-10-10 and later Musk statements); Gen 2 demonstrated 11 DoF (2023-12-12)
parameters.notes: 22-DoF hand demos to date were teleoperated; autonomous dexterity unproven. Human hand ~27 DoF. Hand is the reliability weak point for dusty Mars work (no dust-sealing data published).

parameters.name: Optimus idle power draw (company claim)
parameters.source: Tesla AI Day, 2022-09-30 (company claim): ~100 W sitting
parameters.notes: Upper range covers compute-on standby drain observed in deployed humanoids (15-20% of pack per 8 h idle). Mars cold adds heater load on top - see robots.power.energy_per_work_hour.

parameters.name: Optimus working power draw (claim, with space-humanoid upper bound)
parameters.source: Tesla AI Day 2022 claim: ~500 W brisk walking; upper bound = NASA Valkyrie measured class (1.8 kWh / ~1 h runtime = ~1.8 kW)
parameters.notes: Heavy manipulation/carry likely 600-900 W on Optimus-class actuators. Use 500 W nominal, 1.8 kW conservative for space-hardened variants.

parameters.name: Earth-Mars one-way light time
parameters.source: Orbital geometry; ESA Mars Express blog (2012); NASA ASCEND crewed-Mars comm-delay paper NTRS 20220013418
parameters.notes: Round trip 6-45 min plus DSN scheduling and relay store-and-forward; plus ~2-week conjunction blackout each synod. Rules out Earth-based direct teleoperation of manipulation entirely.

parameters.name: Max round-trip latency for direct manipulation teleoperation
parameters.source: Telerobotics literature: degradation onset ~300 ms RTT, near-collapse of direct control by ~0.7-1 s (Frontiers Robotics & AI 2020 survey; vision-teleop latency studies)
parameters.notes: Above this, operators must use supervisory/scripted control with large productivity loss. Local teleop from hab (RTT < 50 ms) is unconstrained; Earth teleop (RTT >= 360 s) is excluded. This binary is a structural feature of the model.

parameters.name: Productive robot work hours per sol (humanoid, settlement ops)
parameters.source: Estimate: battery bouts 2.5-4 h + 1-3 h charge cycles (Agility Digit 2:1 work:charge company claim, 2024-2025), night cold/power limits, tasking gaps
parameters.notes: Duty cycle ~0.5 of the 24.66 h sol. Upper bound 18 h requires battery swap + nuclear power + night ops. Multiply by fleet availability (robots.maint.fleet_availability) for net robot-hours.

parameters.name: Humanoid recharge time per battery cycle
parameters.source: Estimate: 2.3 kWh pack at ~1C charging with thermal management margin; no Tesla statement found as of 2026-07
parameters.notes: Battery swap (unannounced for Optimus) would reduce effective charge time to minutes at the cost of spare packs (~10-15 kg each) - model as a design option.

parameters.name: Robots supervised per human (local teleop/exception handling, early ops)
parameters.source: Estimate: warehouse AMR/humanoid pilot staffing analogies; Optimus factory ops 2025-2026 still teleop-heavy (Tesla Q4-2025 earnings call, 2026-01-28)
parameters.notes: Ramp toward 10:1 over 2-3 synods as autonomy matures. Binding constraint on unstructured work: teleop slots, not robot count. Pre-crew phase: supervision only from Earth at sol-scale - apply q_rover_science_legacy to unstructured tasks.

parameters.name: Fraction of structured-task robot-hours needing no human in the loop
parameters.source: Estimate: warehouse humanoid deployments run largely autonomous with exception intervention (Agility/GXO 100k-tote milestone, company release 2025-11); Optimus autonomy 'still R&D' per Musk 2026-01-28
parameters.notes: For unstructured repair/dexterous tasks assume <= 0.2 autonomous. Intervention events well-modeled as Poisson with ~15 min median handling time.

parameters.name: Quality multiplier: humanoid vs human, structured tasks (construction/logistics)
parameters.source: Agility Digit @ GXO implied 60-75% of human picker throughput (press analyses of company ROI claims, 2024-2025); Amazon purpose-built stow robot measured 224 vs human 243 units/h (arXiv:2505.04572, 2025)
parameters.notes: Best-evidenced ratio class in the domain. Applies to robot-adapted, repetitive tasks with local supervision available. Add EVA leverage x1.5-3 when the robot-hour substitutes crew EVA time.

parameters.name: Quality multiplier: humanoid vs human, unstructured field/repair tasks
parameters.source: Estimate: no deployed evidence exists; bounded above by structured-task ratios and below by rover field experience; consistent with 2026 state of humanoid autonomy (teleop-dependent for novel manipulation)
parameters.notes: Assumes local teleop available (hab). Without it (pre-crew), use robots.work.q_rover_science_legacy for these tasks. Largest single sensitivity in the domain: settlement-level robot value swings ~5x across this range.

parameters.name: Quality multiplier: sol-scale supervised rover vs human field scientist (flight experience)
parameters.source: Squyres (MER PI) 2005, repeated 2022: what a rover does in a perfect sol, a human field geologist does in ~30-45 s; Crawford, Astron. & Geophys. 2012 (arXiv:1203.6250)
parameters.notes: Computed as ~45 s human / (9 h rover active sol). Documented expert estimate, not a measurement. Governs pre-crew unstructured work under Earth-only supervision; the 350x gap to q_structured quantifies the value of local supervision + structured tasking.

parameters.name: Specialized excavator robot regolith excavation rate (RASSOR/IPEx class)
parameters.source: IPEx mission spec: 10,000 kg at 42 kg/h over 11-day lunar demo (Schuler et al., ASCEND 2024, NASA KSC); RASSOR 2.0 implied continuous rate 66 kg / 0.38 = ~174 kg/h (NTRS 20210011366)
parameters.notes: TRL-5 design values, not flight data. Per ~30-66 kg robot. Lunar-designed; Mars gravity (2.3x lunar) improves traction/dig force, duricrust may resist. Scale linearly with robot mass at ~0.38 kg vehicle mass per kg/h (RASSOR 2.0 figure of merit).

parameters.name: Specific digging energy, bucket-drum excavation (excl. drive/avionics/thermal)
parameters.source: RASSOR 2.0 paper: 4 W per (kg/h) excavation rate = 4 Wh/kg digging-only (NTRS 20210011366); upper range = x2-3 total-system overhead estimate
parameters.notes: Use 4 kWh/t for the digging subsystem (medium confidence, documented), 8-12 kWh/t delivered-to-hopper total system (low). Cross-check: RASSOR 1410 Wh battery + 2.7 t/day implies multiple charge cycles/day - consistent.

parameters.name: Specialized construction robot speedup vs human (single structured task)
parameters.source: Terrestrial analogy, company claims: FBR Hadrian X 200-300 blocks/h (2023 test records) vs human mason 300-500 bricks/day
parameters.notes: Applies only to tasks re-engineered around the robot (block laying, panel placement, regolith 3D printing) - not to humanoids doing human-style construction. Argues the sim should let users buy specialized machines per task category rather than only general humanoids.

parameters.name: Humanoid MTBF, Mars field environment (failures requiring intervention)
parameters.source: Estimate: bounded by FANUC factory-arm vendor claim (~80,000 h MTBF, benign environment) above and IPEx TRL-5 dusty wheel-actuator life test (~1x 11-day mission, bearing failure mode found; Schuler ASCEND 2024) below
parameters.notes: 50-actuator humanoid in abrasive dust and -80C nights will sit far below factory-arm MTBF; practitioner data shows ~88% availability even for terrestrial cells. Least-supported number in the domain - carry the full 100-2000 h range in trade studies. Weibull shape 1.5-2 recommended for L1/L2.

parameters.name: Maintenance labor per 100 robot operating hours
parameters.source: Estimate: MTBF 500 h x MTTR ~4-8 h + preventive (dust cleaning, lube, inspection) ~1-2 h/100 h; consistent with industrial-robot PM schedules (~every 3,000-10,000 h) heavily derated for dust
parameters.notes: At 12 h/sol each robot consumes ~0.36 h/sol technician time; a 10-robot fleet ~= 0.55 crew-equivalents if humans do all of it. Robots self-maintaining at q~0.15 closes part of the loop; verify convergence N*m*(1-r_self*q) < crew budget.

parameters.name: Robot fleet spares mass fraction per Earth-year
parameters.source: Anchored on ISS supportability: ~10 t/yr spares upmass (NTRS 20150003005); Owens & de Weck Mars-One analysis (MIT 2014): spares -> 64% of resupply mass by year 10
parameters.notes: Per 26-month synod carry >= 2.2 yr + reserve: 25-30% of fleet mass per window. Cannibalization of failed units recovers ~50% of spares demand at cost of fleet size. High-wear items: hands, hip/knee actuators, batteries (batteries ~500-1000 cycles = 1-2 Mars years at 1 cycle/sol).

parameters.name: Robot fleet availability (fraction not down for maintenance/repair)
parameters.source: Derived: A = MTBF/(MTBF + MTTR_effective) with MTTR_eff including diagnosis, spares wait, repair queue; consistent with terrestrial robot-cell availability ~88% (The Robot Report)
parameters.notes: Degrade toward 0.7 when spares inventory empties late in a synod or during dust season (solar power constrained + dust-driven failure rate up).

parameters.name: Electrical energy per productive humanoid robot-work-hour (incl. charging loss + Mars thermal)
parameters.source: Derived: 400-500 W claimed working draw (Tesla AI Day 2022) / 0.9 charge efficiency + 50-150 W thermal overhead; upper bound Valkyrie-class measured ~1.8 kW (NASA R5 fact sheet)
parameters.notes: ~7 kWh/sol/robot at 12 h/sol + ~1-3 kWh/night survival heating -> ~9 kWh/sol/robot budget. 100-robot fleet ~ 0.9 MWh/sol ~ 25-40 kW continuous - first-order load for the power domain.

parameters.name: ANCHOR: RASSOR 2.0 excavation throughput
parameters.source: NASA KSC RASSOR 2.0 (NTRS 20210011366): 66 kg robot, 'minimum 2.7 metric tons per day'; 4.2 t/day implied continuous at 174 kg/h
parameters.notes: TRL-4 breadboard, Earth testing, lunar design case. Headline 2.7 t/day embeds ~0.65 duty cycle. One RASSOR-class unit ~= one suited human shoveling full-time, with zero EVA cost.

parameters.name: ANCHOR: ATHLETE heavy cargo robot payload (Earth g prototype)
parameters.source: JPL Robotics ATHLETE program: Gen 1 850 kg vehicle / 300 kg payload; Gen 2 (Tri-ATHLETE) 450 kg payload at Earth g, ~10 km/h max speed
parameters.notes: High confidence as prototype spec (never flown). At Mars 0.379 g the same limbs handle ~2.6x mass -> ~1.2 t class. Template for the 'heavy logistics/cargo' robot category (Starship unloading, module relocation).

parameters.name: ANCHOR: Curiosity average drive distance per driving sol (flight data)
parameters.source: Rankin et al., 'Driving Curiosity: Mars Rover Mobility Trends During the First Seven Years', IEEE Aerospace 2020, NTRS 20220000780; lifetime 36.9 km / 4942 sols = 7.5 m/sol all-sol average (JPL, as of mid-2026)
parameters.notes: MER anchors: top speed 0.05 m/s, ~0.01 m/s under autonomous hazard avoidance; Opportunity lifetime 45.16 km/5111 sols = 8.8 m/sol, record 220 m/sol. These flight rates embody sol-scale Earth supervision + extreme risk aversion - the floor of the productivity scale.

parameters.name: ANCHOR: NASA Valkyrie space-design humanoid average power draw
parameters.source: NASA JSC R5/Valkyrie fact sheet; Radford et al., J. Field Robotics 2015: 125 kg, 44 DoF, 1.8 kWh battery, ~1 h runtime
parameters.notes: The only NASA-built humanoid power datum. 3-4x the Tesla claim - use as the conservative bound for space-hardened humanoid energy modeling.

parameters.name: ANCHOR: Expected corrective maintenance crew time, small surface habitat
parameters.source: Stromgren et al., 'Assessment of Crew Time for Maintenance and Repair Activities for Lunar Surface Missions', IEEE Aerospace 2021, NTRS 20210026843, Table 3 (expected value; 50th pct 9.0 h, 99th pct 70.5 h)
parameters.notes: ISS-data-derived, 2-crew 28-day lunar surface habitat. EVA overhead is 12.52 of the 24.7 h - the component robots most credibly replace. Sizes the 'inspection/maintenance' task-category demand that robot-hours bid against; scale with habitat complexity.

modeling_notes: Core model: robot-hours/sol RH = N * A * H (availability A=0.85, productive hours H=12 h/sol per humanoid), converted to human-equivalent hours per task category via quality multipliers q_c: excavation = rate-based (42-174 kg/h per RASSOR/IPEx-class unit, 4 kWh/t digging + 2-3x system overhead), construction q=0.5, logistics q=0.6, inspection q=0.8, greenhouse tending q=0.25, complex repair q=0.15; multiply q by 1.5-3x EVA-leverage when robot-hours substitute crew EVA (EVA overhead was ~50% of the lunar-habitat maintenance crew-time estimate). Supervision is a hard constraint: Earth-Mars light time (3-22 min one-way) excludes direct teleoperation (collapses above ~0.7 s RTT), so pre-crew unstructured work must use rover-class multipliers (q~0.001, Squyres/Crawford), while local teleop from the hab restores warehouse-class ratios - model pre-crew and crewed phases with different q-vectors and a robots-per-supervisor limit (3, ramping to 10). Close two loops: (1) maintenance - robots consume 3 h tech labor per 100 robot-h, 10%/yr fleet mass in spares (26-month synod logistics: carry 25-30% fleet mass per window), fleet availability degrades when spares run out; (2) energy - 0.6 kWh per productive robot-hour (~9 kWh/sol/robot incl. night heating) charged against the power domain, with dust season reducing both available energy and robot availability simultaneously. ALL Optimus figures are company claims (dated 2022-2026, none independently verified; Musk 2026-01-28: fleet 'primarily for learning'); treat 57 kg / 2.3 kWh as design-class inputs and runtime/payload/speed claims as upper bounds. Recommended fidelity tiers: L0 scalar (10.2 robot-h/sol/robot * q_c), L1 sol-indexed with dust/power/aging/spares dynamics and autonomy learning curve, L2 sub-sol SOC-thermal-reliability simulation with task queues and teleop-slot scheduling (equations in robots.md).
key_findings: No Tesla Optimus performance figure is field-demonstrated as of July 2026: mass 57 kg and battery 2.3 kWh are stable company claims (2022-2023), but the 8 h runtime, 68 kg deadlift, and 2.2 m/s walk claims are unverified, and Musk himself (Q4-2025 earnings call, 2026-01-28) called the >1,000-unit Fremont fleet 'primarily for learning, not productive tasks'.

Physics excludes Earth-based teleoperation of manipulation on Mars: round-trip light time is 6-45 min vs a ~0.7 s tolerance for direct teleop; local teleoperation from the habitat (<50 ms) is fully viable, so robot productivity is structurally different before vs after crew arrival - pre-crew unstructured work inherits rover-class rates (~0.001 human-equivalent), crewed-phase structured work reaches warehouse-class rates (0.3-0.9).

The robot:human work-rate ratio spans three orders of magnitude with task structure: 0.92 for purpose-built stationary manipulation (Amazon stow, measured), 0.5-0.75 for humanoid structured logistics (Digit@GXO, company-derived), 0.03-0.3 for unstructured field work (no evidence, speculative), 0.001 for sol-scale supervised rover science (Squyres, flight experience) - a single scalar 'robot = X% of human' is the wrong model form.

Specialized robots beat humanoids for the biggest Mars workloads: a 66 kg RASSOR-class excavator moves >=2.7 t regolith/day (~4 kWh/t digging energy) and a Hadrian-X-class machine lays blocks 5-20x faster than a human - the sim should let specialized machines own excavation/construction while humanoids cover logistics, inspection, and greenhouse/maintenance tasks.

A defensible planning point for one Optimus-class robot is ~10 productive h/sol (12 h duty cycle x 0.85 availability) at ~0.6 kWh per work-hour (~9 kWh/sol including night heating); at q=0.5 that is ~0.8 crew-equivalents for structured outdoor work with zero EVA overhead - but the same robot is only ~0.15 crew-equivalents at unstructured tasks.

Maintenance is the tail risk: field MTBF for a 50-actuator humanoid in abrasive dust at -80C nights is unknown (est. 500 h, range 100-2000 h; NASA's TRL-5 dusty excavator actuator barely survived 1x an 11-day mission in life test), and ISS-derived logistics analysis says spares grow toward ~64% of resupply mass over a decade - each 26-month synod should carry 25-30% of fleet mass in spares.

Flight-heritage planetary robot rates (Curiosity 28.9 m per driving sol; Opportunity 8.8 m/sol lifetime; ~1 cm/s under autonomous hazard avoidance) are 2-4 orders of magnitude below what the same hardware could do with local supervision - the gap is command-cycle and risk posture, not actuation - which is the central argument for why settlement robots with local human oversight can be modeled at near-terrestrial productivity for structured tasks.
